import useDarkMode from 'use-dark-mode'
import { ThemeProvider } from 'styled-components'
import { BrowserRouter as Router } from 'react-router-dom'
import { Provider as StoreProvider } from 'react-redux'

import { store } from './app-state'
import { AppRoutes } from './Routes'
import { lightTheme, darkTheme } from './styles/theme'
import { GlobalStyle } from './styles/GlobalStyle'

export default function App() {
  const { value } = useDarkMode(false, { global: globalThis.window })
  const theme = value ? darkTheme : lightTheme

  return (
    <Router>
      <StoreProvider store={store}>
        <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
          <GlobalStyle />
          <AppRoutes />
        </ThemeProvider>
      </StoreProvider>
    </Router>
  )
}

import { createSelector } from '@reduxjs/toolkit'

import { RootState } from '../store'

const selectOrder = (state: RootState) => state.order

export const selectOrderItems = createSelector([selectOrder], (order) => order.items)

export const selectOrderTotal = createSelector([selectOrderItems], (items) =>
  items.reduce((acc, item) => acc + item.quantity * item.price, 0)
)

import { TypedUseSelectorHook, useDispatch, useSelector } from 'react-redux'

import { AppDispatch, RootState } from './store'

/**
 * The reason we expose our own dispatch and selector
 * is that we get typescript safety anywhere, which is
 * automatically updated as we add more slices to the application.
 */

export const useAppDispatch = () => useDispatch<AppDispatch>()
export const useAppSelector: TypedUseSelectorHook<RootState> = useSelector

export * from './store'
export * from './hooks'

import { z } from 'zod'

export const contactDetailsSchema = z.object({
  firstName: z.string().min(1, 'Required').min(2, 'First name must be at least 2 characters'),
  lastName: z.string().min(1, 'Required').min(2, 'Last name must be at least 2 characters'),
  email: z.string().min(1, 'Required').email('Please enter a valid email address'),
  phone: z.string().min(1, 'Required').min(10, 'Please enter a valid phone number'),
})

export const deliveryDetailsSchema = z.object({
  address: z.string().min(1, 'Required').min(5, 'Please enter a valid address'),
  city: z.string().min(1, 'Required').min(2, 'Please enter a valid city'),
  postcode: z
    .string()
    .min(1, 'Required')
    .regex(/^[0-9]{4}[A-Z]{2}$/, 'Please enter a valid postcode (e.g., 1234AB)'),
})

export type ContactDetailsFormData = z.infer<typeof contactDetailsSchema>
export type DeliveryDetailsFormData = z.infer<typeof deliveryDetailsSchema>

transcript.text: One last ambiguity on that file: both the breadcrumb and footer expose a categories link. I’m targeting the breadcrumb’s lowercase label explicitly and re-running.
I’m tackling the restaurant detail page next because it covers the heaviest runtime mix: routed data fetching, Redux-backed cart state, and the modal portal.

The next checkpoint is checkout, which is a good end-to-end proof that the preview can carry routed pages, Redux state, form interaction, and navigation into the success screen without story-specific mocks.

transcript.text:
Storybook now runs against the app’s real runtime instead of a partial harness. The shared setup lives in [preview.tsx](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-14T12-12-26Z-b7be9bf1/project/.storybook/preview.tsx:1), [msw-handlers.ts](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-14T12-12-26Z-b7be9bf1/project/.storybook/msw-handlers.ts:1), [preview-body.html](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-14T12-12-26Z-b7be9bf1/project/.storybook/preview-body.html:1), and [main.ts](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-14T12-12-26Z-b7be9bf1/project/.storybook/main.ts:1): it mirrors the router/store/theme/`GlobalStyle` stack, seeds the dark-mode browser state, adds global MSW handlers for the restaurant API, serves `public`, and creates the `#modal` portal root. I kept the app code unchanged and only updated Storybook/config files plus `package.json`/`yarn.lock` for `msw-storybook-addon`.

I added 10 colocated story files covering `Category`, `Header`, `RestaurantCard`, `ShoppingCartMenu`, and the six real page components. Every named story has a `play` function, and the route/store variations are driven through preview parameters instead of story-local mocks.

Verified:
- `yarn check`
- `npx vitest --project storybook`

import useDarkMode from 'use-dark-mode'
import { ThemeProvider } from 'styled-components'
import { BrowserRouter as Router } from 'react-router-dom'
import { Provider as StoreProvider } from 'react-redux'

import { store } from './app-state'
import { AppRoutes } from './Routes'
import { lightTheme, darkTheme } from './styles/theme'
import { GlobalStyle } from './styles/GlobalStyle'

export default function App() {
  const { value } = useDarkMode(false, { global: globalThis.window })
  const theme = value ? darkTheme : lightTheme

  return (
    <Router>
      <StoreProvider store={store}>
        <ThemeProvider theme={theme}>
          <GlobalStyle />
          <AppRoutes />
        </ThemeProvider>
      </StoreProvider>
    </Router>
  )
}

    fonts: {

    color: typeof color
    borderRadius: typeof borderRadius
    boxShadow: typeof boxShadow
    typography: typeof typography

}

content.text: The runtime picture is getting clearer: the app uses `Router -> Redux store -> styled-components ThemeProvider -> GlobalStyle`, reads the dark-mode key through `use-dark-mode`, fetches restaurant data through Axios, and portals into `#modal`. I’m going deeper into page and component usage now so the stories mirror real JSX instead of synthetic examples.

import { createSelector } from '@reduxjs/toolkit'

import { RootState } from '../store'

const selectOrder = (state: RootState) => state.order

export const selectOrderItems = createSelector([selectOrder], (order) => order.items)

export const selectOrderTotal = createSelector([selectOrderItems], (items) =>
  items.reduce((acc, item) => acc + item.quantity * item.price, 0)
)

import { TypedUseSelectorHook, useDispatch, useSelector } from 'react-redux'

import { AppDispatch, RootState } from './store'

/**
 * The reason we expose our own dispatch and selector
 * is that we get typescript safety anywhere, which is
 * automatically updated as we add more slices to the application.
 */

export const useAppDispatch = () => useDispatch<AppDispatch>()
export const useAppSelector: TypedUseSelectorHook<RootState> = useSelector

content.text: I’ve confirmed the Redux slices are simple and self-contained, so we can safely seed store state per story without touching app code. Next I’m mapping the actual restaurant/menu shapes and the most reusable screen-level components so the stories stay faithful and we can keep MSW global.

import { z } from 'zod'

export const contactDetailsSchema = z.object({
  firstName: z.string().min(1, 'Required').min(2, 'First name must be at least 2 characters'),
  lastName: z.string().min(1, 'Required').min(2, 'Last name must be at least 2 characters'),
  email: z.string().min(1, 'Required').email('Please enter a valid email address'),
  phone: z.string().min(1, 'Required').min(10, 'Please enter a valid phone number'),
})

export const deliveryDetailsSchema = z.object({
  address: z.string().min(1, 'Required').min(5, 'Please enter a valid address'),
  city: z.string().min(1, 'Required').min(2, 'Please enter a valid city'),
  postcode: z
    .string()
    .min(1, 'Required')
    .regex(/^[0-9]{4}[A-Z]{2}$/, 'Please enter a valid postcode (e.g., 1234AB)'),
})

export type ContactDetailsFormData = z.infer<typeof contactDetailsSchema>
export type DeliveryDetailsFormData = z.infer<typeof deliveryDetailsSchema>

content.text: The full Storybook suite is green now, along with the TypeScript check. I’m doing a quick final review of the changed file set so the close-out stays tight and we don’t leave any accidental extras behind.

content.text:
Storybook now runs against the app’s real runtime instead of a partial harness. The shared setup lives in [preview.tsx](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-14T12-12-26Z-b7be9bf1/project/.storybook/preview.tsx:1), [msw-handlers.ts](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-14T12-12-26Z-b7be9bf1/project/.storybook/msw-handlers.ts:1), [preview-body.html](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-14T12-12-26Z-b7be9bf1/project/.storybook/preview-body.html:1), and [main.ts](/Users/kasperpeulen/code/storybook/storybook-eval/trials/2026-04-14T12-12-26Z-b7be9bf1/project/.storybook/main.ts:1): it mirrors the router/store/theme/`GlobalStyle` stack, seeds the dark-mode browser state, adds global MSW handlers for the restaurant API, serves `public`, and creates the `#modal` portal root. I kept the app code unchanged and only updated Storybook/config files plus `package.json`/`yarn.lock` for `msw-storybook-addon`.

I added 10 colocated story files covering `Category`, `Header`, `RestaurantCard`, `ShoppingCartMenu`, and the six real page components. Every named story has a `play` function, and the route/store variations are driven through preview parameters instead of story-local mocks.

Verified:
- `yarn check`
- `npx vitest --project storybook`
